data.title: Amazon's Stalemate: Earning Income While Waiting For The Next Catalyst
data.description:
Since I last reviewed Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) (TSX:AMZN:CA) and downgraded it to a Hold (after reaping in ~31% profits), it has underperformed the S&P 500 by 9 percentage points. Since July, the S&P 500 has rallied by ~6%, while Amazon is down by 3% from the time I wrote. Not a huge underperformance, but it feels good to get the timing right so perfectly.

I generally believe that Amazon is a core portfolio allocation, and such stocks should not be judged for a few percentage points here or there in the short term. The reason why I downgraded Amazon to a Hold in July was part profit booking (having timed a decent entry in April) and part lack of any near term catalyst visibility.

Has Anything Changed?

Overall, the reasons for the downgrade were primarily a lack of momentum that Amazon often suffers from in phases. Since July, lack of any near term catalyst has rendered an even more benign profile to the stock. The valuations have hardly moved from the July levels of ~14x in EV to forward EBITDA ratio terms. That is not expensive, but not the usual value territory (around ~11-12x) where Amazon Buys have been more rewarded in the recent past.

The macro environment has changed though - there has been a 25 bps rate cut in the interim that fuels hopes of supportive equity valuations - particularly high tech valuations. Although valuations may be breathing a bit easier, there is enough left in terms of macro pressures to keep the broader market pressure factor roughly intact from where we were in July. Tariff concerns, that were starting to work through negotiations and ceasefire signals, have again landed in uncertain territory. Consumer spending pressures are still very much a threat as well.

Several companies have reported decent numbers in Q2, a quarter where tariff impacts should have started to show up - easing concerns around the extent of impact of tariffs. The tariff concerns seem to be absorbed a lot better today (barring the new China developments). However, uncertainties are still a drag and supply chain disruptions are concerns even if the quantum of financial impact looks slightly better than initially expected. You can read a simplistic tariff model and its expected range of impact on Arista here. The impact is certainly not a difference between a company turning financially fragile from profit making - but a dent on supply chain and geopolitical positioning is a drag, at least for an e-commerce player like Amazon. So overall, while the macro environment has changed, an optimal macro environment required to support a Buy thesis has not emerged.

On the business front, AWS grew by 17.5% in Q2 2025, a slight improvement over the 17% growth seen in Q1, but still short of my 20-25% growth expectations that becomes a rerating trigger. The advertising growth of 19% in Q2 is a continuation of strong momentum in an expanding segment beyond AWS - I expect advertising to remain strong going into H2 2025 and the foreseeable future. Advertising is a silver lining and a decent supporting factor, but not thesis altering on its own. The core thesis-altering factor in my view is returning growth in AWS and that is yet to be seen.

AWS should keep growing at levels healthy enough to allay concerns of competitive pressures from Azure and Google Cloud. Then there are other catalysts that have not been on fire and belied market expectations of a growth catalyst in the near term. Amazon's healthcare unit is yet to establish itself as a growing and profitable unit despite years of investments, including acquisitions. Kuiper prospects have also been dampened due to multiple delays pushing back commercial monetization to not before 2026.

Playing the Stalemate

There have been very few positive catalysts in adjacent businesses. AWS is waiting for a rebound, and international profitability remains elusive for the e-commerce segment. While the story is clearly bland now and the wait for a material catalyst continues, everything is not negative. There have been developments that support Amazon from further material downsides. AWS is still not in concern territory, and advertising growth continues to impress, for example.

Amazon is in a zone where I think it will continue to trade between $200-250 till a catalyst emerges. Amazon has a habit of oscillating between psychological round numbers before breaking into the next range when catalysts emerge or momentum starts picking up. This stalemate presents a perfect opportunity to implement a "wheel-like" strategy. A strategy of setting up monthly put sell positions at the support point of $200 (or higher for aggressive investors) - earning premiums if Amazon continues to trade above the $200 mark. Any dip below $200 presents a discounted entry point for investors who have a bullish long term thesis on Amazon (which I do).

Amazon's long term thesis is sufficiently supported just by the scale of its e-commerce presence, access to data that can be leveraged across its adjacent businesses (targeted ads, healthcare suggestions, etc.) and a flywheel effect due to its wide ecosystem. Add to that the fact that valuations are still conducive - Amazon has had the least valuation explosion among other tech names in the Magnificent Seven pack and even within the defensive tech pack (like MSFT and GOOG). Historically, pullbacks are the best times to enter Amazon, particularly beyond nearest support round numbers - $200 in this case.

So assignment at strikes of $200 is a discounted entry in the wheel strategy, I suggest - long term investors will find those entry points value supportive. In fact the reason I suggest the wheel strategy for Amazon is because I don't mind owning it at $200 at all.

The $200 put option is now trading at ~$4.4 for a mid-November expiry (a month away). That is 2.2% premium earned in just a month (~26% annualized). The premium collected could go up if Amazon nears $200 and go down to up to ~0.5%, if Amazon trades at $240-250. Still the average income from such a strategy is lucrative enough and can be dynamically adjusted to generate returns anywhere between 5-30% within a year. The worst outcome in this trade is an assignment at $200, which is a great entry in my view.

The income potential is not only higher than Amazon's ~15% gains in the past one year, but the most appropriate positioning given the fundamental and technical drags in the stock. The other risk of this strategy is Amazon's breakaway from the trading range of $200-250, leading to an opportunity loss. That is a lesser concern as of today, as investors can ideally switch to a Buy Amazon position from this strategy, whenever catalysts or momentum emerge. There is none at the moment.

Another way to play Amazon's stalemate is using the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (AMZY). This reduces drawdown risks (again gives a discounted entry point indirectly if Amazon falls below $200). However, AMZY pays out capital aggressively and needs reinvestments to stay fully invested. Fundamentally, AMZY works on a call writing principle - in contrast to my cash-secured put-selling suggestion. There are nuances between the two routes, but both could work for Amazon's strict trading range.

Summarizing, Amazon is a core portfolio holding at the right prices. Today, we are very near those price levels, but a lack of catalysts and momentum justifies an income oriented approach over a full conviction Buy for conservative traders. As catalysts emerge, investors may switch over to a full fledged Buy, or earn decent income through the option enabled strategies I have mentioned. For now, I keep Amazon a Hold and ride this period with an income outlook of 5-30%, even if the range is not broken or the lack of catalysts persist.

Risks to the Thesis

The only risk to the thesis is if Amazon breaks out of the $200-250 trading range. On the downside, there is no risk apart from a buying opportunity at $200 or slightly below (given the premiums earned). Of course, AWS headwinds or any extreme loss of leadership position in its e-commerce moat could weaken Amazon's Buy at $200 thesis too. But that looks like a far-fetched case to plan for, looking at today's dynamics. We are talking about situations like a low double digit AWS growth or flat e-commerce sales for a couple of quarters. Even in those cases, a Buy below $200 is better risk adjusted than now.

The upside opportunity loss is a more real risk and very likely given Amazon's relatively subdued performance in the past few years. Any advertising, adjacent business growth, AWS rebound or value unlocking overall due to the Amazon flywheel could easily push Amazon to the next trading range of $250-$300. In this case too, investors following a wheel strategy can still end up with 5-10% income for the year, but lose out 20-30% upside opportunity. Of course, I have maintained that investors should switch to a traditional long position when catalysts appear - there are no such signs as of now.

Earnings Preview

Amazon's recent earnings history has been positive surprises for the past few quarters and I do not see any change to that trend in Q3 2025. However, I feel the beat will be subdued given expectations on holiday season ramp up, continued enterprise AI spending will be balanced by uncertain tariff impacts and consumer demand weakening. How much the Prime Day and promotions affect the e-commerce number will be interesting to see. If e-commerce sales decelerate to anywhere below 10% in North America, I would consider that a negative given the Prime Day quarter expectations. I am looking at a non GAAP EPS slightly higher than expectations at $1.6 and revenue to clock around $180b (supported by mostly positive FX impacts from international sales).

Beyond headline EPS and revenue numbers, I will keep a keen watch on AWS growth - betting on continued growth from 17% YoY in Q1 to 17.5% in Q2 to 18% now. Any miss there could weaken sentiment on AWS. I am also building in an expectation for growth above 20% in advertising - so any miss there will also likely dampen sentiment.

Guidance and updates on Project Kuiper and capex spending in general will be important. Any management commentary on tariff negotiations and progress, particularly with a new round of uncertainties on China tariffs, will be important to align expectations going ahead.

Any AWS growth of 20%+ could lead to potential rally and affect the thesis - investors should switch from the wheel strategy to an outright Buy position - as this will be a solid rerating catalyst. I don't see the odds of such positive catalysts as very high. On the flip side, any disappointment may push Amazon below $200 and open up fresh long-term entries (per the thesis).

data.title: Bank OZK: Regional Bank Fears Create Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) plunged 7% on Thursday as the regional bank sector was slammed amid fears of increasing credit losses. Firms like Jefferies (JEF), Fifth Third (FITB), Western Alliance (WAL), and Zions (ZION) have all flagged losses, creating genuine worry about private credit. While OZK has a much different loan book focused in commercial real estate ("CRE"), that did not help shares. After the bell, the company then reported mixed results, which may not benefit sentiment. I last covered shares in July, rating the stock a "strong buy," but performance has been disappointing, with OZK losing 10%. Given updated financials and new macro worries, now is a good time to revisit shares. I remain bullish.

In the company's third quarter, Bank OZK earned $1.59, which was $0.10 below consensus, even as revenue rose by 6% to $450 million. EPS was up 1% sequentially and about 3% from a year ago, reaching a new record. Some of this miss was driven by a smaller asset base, given elevated repayments, which I view as a net positive given lingering credit concerns. Additionally, provisions for losses ticked up $13 million to $48 million as OZK built reserves. Higher charge-offs were not the driver of the reserve build. This increase was about a $0.10 detriment to sequential EPS, accounting for most of the miss. Provision levels do remain relatively low.

Strong deposit activity supports NIM

Net interest income rose $34 million from a year ago to $414 million thanks to aggressive asset growth. This was also up $17 million sequentially. I expect most of this gain to be reversed in Q4 as the impact of Fed rate cuts filters through, likely resulting in about $400 million of NII. I would note that management cited a "goal" of growing NII next year, but I view a "goal" as less confident than actual guidance. Its net interest margin ("NIM") fell 1 bp sequentially, and it is 20 bps lower over the past year. I expect a similar amount of NIM compression over the next year, given my expectation for 3-4 Fed rate cuts.

Turning to its balance sheet, OZK ended the quarter with $34 billion in deposits, up $500 million in Q3 and $3.5 billion from a year ago. OZK has been an excellent accumulator of deposits, aided by above-average interest rates. This strong deposit growth has helped to fuel its excellent loan growth while ensuring the company maintains adequate liquidity. While the absolute cost of its deposits is relatively high, they are down 67 bps from a year ago, an excellent pull-through of Fed rate cuts. I expect deposit costs to fall in Q4 given Fed rate cuts, but given CDs, deposit rates will likely take a bit more time to react than loan yields.

CRE repayments surged in Q3

Bank OZK had $32.9 billion in loans, up nearly $3 billion this year. However, shockingly for a bank that has grown loans rapidly, they actually declined by $0.2 billion in the quarter. That is because the bank received a record $2.4 billion in real estate principal payments vs just $0.7 billion in originations (the slowest in over five years). In past quarters, management had said that payments would accelerate materially in H2, but this exceeded my expectations. Capital market access for CRE borrowers improved meaningfully over the past ~4-5 months, and that is now hitting OZK's results, after low prepayments exaggerated loan growth in H1.

I view these payments as a clear positive. There has been ongoing concern about the CRE-heavy nature of OZK's loan book, and it has been a very slow process diversifying. These payments help to accelerate that effort. Additionally, payments of this magnitude go a long way to showcasing OZK's strong underwriting and the fact that not all CRE exposure is bad. This development adds to my comfort in OZK's credit quality as well as my view that the valuation overhang from it is not entirely justified. Nearly, all of this portfolio was underwritten post-COVID, consistent with the company's strong track record of repayment.

As you can see below, OZK has about 58% of its loan book in real estate. This is still extremely high with most of the sector below 35%. However, it actually has been falling considerably. Due to these high Q2 repayments, the real estate share fell by 230 bps sequentially, and it is 10% below its all-time high. I expect real estate exposure to fall below 50% by late next year. Given OZK's specialized underwriting skills in real estate, I would expect the share to stabilize close to 50%.

Credit quality is solid

While commercial real estate was once seen as a liability, this exposure actually may make OZK appear comparatively safe. With the recent spate of high-profile corporate defaults including Tricolor and First Brands, suddenly, business lending appears riskier. This would especially be the case if the economy rolls over into recession. Declining interest rates also helps to support CRE valuations, making it easier to refinance. Some CRE asset classes, like office and select multifamily, have clear challenges, but if private credit is really the next epicenter of losses, OZK may be fairly well positioned compared to peers, a reversal of the more recent perception.

The company has $150 million in non-accrual loans and $79 million in foreclosure, so reserves are about 3x this, a healthy level. Importantly, on its real estate portfolio, the loan-to-value is just 46% on average, greatly insulating the company from potential losses. This is a key reason I am comfortable with OZK's CRE exposure. It now has $680 million in reserves, with reserves up $14 million sequentially. As you can see below, OZK has been steadily building reserves while also reporting record profits. Net charge-offs were a muted 41bps.

Capital is strong, enabling shareholder returns

I would also note that unlike other banks that have been saddled with large losses on their investment securities portfolio, OZK has a relatively small portfolio of just $2.8 billion. The portfolio also yields 4.25%, up from 3.00% a year ago as low-yielding securities matured. I do not expect much further yield appreciation, and I expect the portfolio to remain in the $2.5-$3.0 billion range depending on deposit and loan growth.

Importantly, Bank OZK has an excellent capital position with an 11.55% tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio. This capital position provides plenty of flexibility for ongoing asset growth, not to mention ensuring the security of its capital returns to investors, while also providing a cushion in case loan losses do come in above expectations.

In its commentary, management suggested that buybacks could accelerate under its $200 million buyback program. Its share count is down only 0.7% over the past year, speaking to how little repurchase activity there has been to this point. Additionally, OZK has increased its dividend for 61 straight quarters, and shares now have a 3.6% dividend yield.

Shares are too cheap to ignore

Back in July, I was targeting $6.25-$6.50 in EPS this year. With these results, I see EPS coming in at the low-end of my range, or about $6.25, due to slightly higher reserves and lower loan balances, due to strong repayment activity. OZK has a $45 tangible book value, and I believe this can help put a floor under shares, making now an attractive entry point. I previously thought OZK could trade at 10x earnings, still below many peers.

With increased worry about credit in general, I see multiple compression pressures across the regional banking complex. Still, OZK is below 8x, which I view as extremely cheap, especially as its loan book is not centered at all in private credit or business lending. As such, I still view OZK as a top pick in the sector and reiterate my strong buy. Even at 9x, shares can trade into the upper-$50s, and some incremental buybacks can provide support. This regional bank scare is creating opportunities for value investors, and Bank OZK should be bought.

data.title: Fed Chair Powell: Balance Sheet Reduction May End Soon
data.description:
By Mike Maharrey

In another sign that we are entering an era of even looser monetary policy, Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell hinted that balance sheet reduction is about to come to an end.

“Our long-stated plan is to stop balance sheet runoff when reserves are somewhat above the level we judge consistent with ample reserve conditions. We may approach that point in coming months, and we are closely monitoring a wide range of indicators to inform this decision.”

In practice, this means the central bank will stop reducing its holdings of Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities, maintaining the size of its balance sheet at the current level.

Balance sheet reduction, or quantitative tightening (QT), pulls liquidity out of the financial system by reducing bank reserves and shifting government debt financing to the private sector. This tightens funding conditions and market debt. It is effectively deflationary.

Powell said, “Some signs have begun to emerge that liquidity conditions are gradually tightening,” thereby necessitating the end of QT.

More significantly, an end balance sheet reduction would take some pressure off the Treasury market, as the central bank would have to purchase new bonds as the old ones mature. In effect, this would create additional artificial demand in the Treasury market.

Powell didn’t provide a timeline for ending balance sheet runoff.

The Fed chair also hinted that more interest rate cuts are in the cards. He also alluded to the tightrope the central bank is trying to walk.

“If we move too quickly, then we may leave the inflation job unfinished and have to come back later and finish it. If we move too slowly, there may be unnecessary losses, painful losses, in the employment market. So, we’re in the difficult situation of balancing those two things.”

Fed Balance Sheet Operations Explained

Quantitative Easing

Interest rate policy gets the most attention, but the Federal Reserve balance sheet is an important monetary policy tool. In effect, it serves as a direct pipeline to the money supply.

When the Fed buys assets – primarily U.S. Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities – it does so with money created out of thin air. Those assets go on the balance sheet, and the new money gets injected into the financial system and ultimately the broader economy. This process is known as quantitative easing (QE).

Before the 2008 financial crisis and Great Recession, the balance sheet was just over $900 billion. By the end of the pandemic era, it stood at just under $9 trillion. In other words, the Fed pumped over $8 trillion into the economy in 14 years through QE.

We can see the impact of QE in the trajectory of the money supply. Note that it declined after the Fed began QT and cut interest rates in 2022, but it has since resumed its relentless climb.

When Ben Bernanke launched the first round of QE at the onset of the Great Recession, he assured Congress that the Fed was not monetizing the debt. (When the Fed buys a bond with money created out of thin air, it is effectively turning that debt into money - thus the term "debt monetization.")

He said the difference between debt monetization and the Fed’s policy was that the central bank was not providing a permanent source of financing. He said the Treasuries would only remain on the Fed’s balance sheet temporarily.

He assured Congress that once the crisis was over, the Federal Reserve would sell the bonds it bought during the emergency. That never happened. And then the Fed doubled down, expanding the balance sheet by nearly $5 trillion during the pandemic. This is, by definition, inflation.

As former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh explained in an op-ed published by the Wall Street Journal last year, there is a direct connection between this monetary expansion and price inflation.

“The monetary base is up 60 percent since the pandemic. Another measure of money, M2, is up 36 percent in the past four years. The inflation surge in the same period– cumulatively about 22 percent– shouldn’t have been a surprise.”

Quantitative Tightening

Quantitative tightening unwinds quantitative easing. As Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities mature, the central bank doesn’t replace them, thereby shrinking the size of the balance sheet over time.

Since the Fed isn’t replacing the bonds, the U.S. Treasury must raise money from the public to redeem the central bank. Of course, Uncle Sam is broke. That means the Treasury Department has to sell a new bond on the open market – essentially borrowing money to pay off the old debt.

When a private individual or entity buys a Treasury, they typically use cash, reducing their bank deposit. In effect, this lowers overall financial system reserves and shifts more debt financing to the public.

The Fed announced a balance sheet reduction plan in March 2022 when it could no longer convince everybody that price inflation was “transitory.” The plan wasn’t exactly ambitious, given the amount of inflation it created during the pandemic.

If the Fed followed the blueprint (and it didn’t), it would take 7.8 years for the Fed to shrink its balance sheet back to pre-pandemic levels. This doesn't even account for the trillions added in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis.

And of course, the central bank didn’t stick with the plan. The Fed slowed the pace of balance sheet reduction in May 2024. From its peak, the Fed has run around $2.4 trillion off the balance sheet. That sounds impressive, but it only represents about half of the increase during the pandemic alone.

Powell said that the Fed has no plans to reduce the balance sheet back to pre-pandemic levels. So much for Bernanke’s promise.

Why End QT Now?

If expanding the money supply is inflationary, it logically follows that to truly beat down price inflation, the Fed needed to shrink the balance sheet substantially. Theoretically, it should have reduced the size of the balance sheet at least to the neighborhood of pre-crisis levels, as Bernanke promised.

Rate hikes aren’t enough to wring all the liquidity out of the economy. Warsh made this very point in his op-ed, explaining that if the Fed is serious about taming inflation, it needs to significantly reduce the size of the balance sheet. In other words, it needs to undo the money creation of the past decade-plus.

It didn’t. And it won’t. So, if inflation isn’t at the 2 percent target, why did the Fed start winding down its balance sheet reduction in June? And why are they talking about ending it altogether today?

Probably because they learned their lesson in 2018. After the 2008 financial crisis, the Fed maintained its bloated balance sheet until 2017.

When it finally made an effort to reduce the balance sheet, it didn’t last very long. Why? Because the monetary tightening was about to break the economy and the financial system.

The stock market crashed in the fall of 2018, and there were recession warning signs flashing in the economy. The Fed put rate hikes on hold in December that year and started cutting rates in 2019.

In August 2019, the Fed reversed course on balance sheet reduction and launched a new round of quantitative easing as the short-term lending markets went haywire. Fed officials refused to call it QE. They even denied that it was QE. But whatever you want to call it, the result was the same as QE. The balance sheet began to expand again.

The Fed was able to shrink the balance sheet more this time around because the monetary expansion was bigger and faster during the pandemic than it was in 2008. But the central bankers know they’re playing with fire. They know that this economy can’t run without easy money. And that’s exactly why they’ve already slowed their roll even though they continue to claim inflation isn’t dead yet.

The National Debt Problem

While Powell & company would never admit it, the central bank has no choice but to end balance sheet reduction due to the federal government’s borrowing and spending problem. When Bernanke claimed the central bank wasn’t monetizing the debt, he was either hopelessly naive or he was lying.

In effect, the Fed supports the government’s borrowing by creating artificial demand for Treasuries. This drives the price higher and yields lower, driving the U.S. government's borrowing costs down.

If the Fed weren’t holding so much U.S. debt, Treasury interest rates would be substantially higher. An end to QT makes sense given that the federal government is paying more than $1 trillion in interest expense right now.

While an end to balance sheet reduction won’t inject newly created money into the economy, it will reduce the number of bonds the government has to sell on the open market, thereby easing supply, raising Treasury prices, and putting downward pressure on yields.

Simply ending balance sheet reduction might not be enough. Given falling global demand for Treasuries and persistently high yields, the central bank may well have to launch quantitative easing again in the near future to press its thumb harder on the Treasury market. In effect, this would be a return to money printing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ultragenyx: Rough Road Ahead Despite Growing Revenues And Catalysts
data.description:
This is my third Ultragenyx (NASDAQ:RARE) article, following 03/2024's "Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical Earnings: No Visible Path To Financial Success Yet". Ultragenx has had a rough go since the publication of No Visible Path, with its price since knocked down ~40%.

Ultragenyx has built a mix of approved and late stage molecules over its 15 year existence

Overview

In its latest 10-K Ultragenyx includes the following graphic listing its four approved molecules and its six in clinical development:

Its 10-K sets out the following table of disaggregated total revenues which it has generated in recent years from its four approved molecules:

Slide 32 from Ultragenyx's 09/2025 website Corporate Presentation provides a graphic presentation of its approved therapies' revenues over the years along with its guidance for 2025:

CRYSVITA as will be discussed in greater detail below is its crucial cash cow. In addition it has its three other approved therapies two of which are in solid growth mode. In addition to reviewing its approved therapies I also look into its most advanced projects in development.

CRYSVITA

In 2018, Ultragenyx and its collaborator Kyowa Kirin (OTCPK:KYKOF) announced FDA approval of CRYSVITA (burosumab-twza) for the treatment of X-linked hypophosphatemia [XLH] in adult and pediatric patients one year of age and older. Subsequently in 2020 CRYSVITA was approved to treat certain instances of fibroblast growth factor 23 [FGF23]-related hypophosphatemia in tumor-induced osteomalacia [TIO] associated with phosphaturic mesenchymal tumors.

Ultragenyx's annual CRYSVITA product sales revenues as reported in its 10-K's have nearly doubled every year since 2020's sales of ~$10,000. The trajectory of other CRYSVITA revenue categories variously referred to as CRYSVITA royalty revenue and CRYSVITA collaboration revenue in Profit-Share Territory is more difficult to assess.

As shown above in 2024 CRYSVITA collaboration revenue in Profit-Share Territory fell to nil from ~$71 million in 2023 and ~$215 million in 2022. Its table below of disaggregated revenue from its 10-K issued in 2023 shows significant revenues of this type generated during years 2020-2022:

During its Q2/2025 earnings Call CFO Horn reiterated its prior revenue guidance indicating that:

.... CRYSVITA revenue is expected to be between $460 million and $480 million, which includes all regions and all forms of CRYSVITA revenue to Ultragenyx. This range represents 12% to 17% growth over 2024. (emphasis added)

Bottom line is that CRYSVITA should remain in growth mode for the balance of 2025. Its future trajectory will depend on a variety of factors most significantly its competitive matrix and its intellectual property [IP] protection.

In terms of competition its 10-K advises as follows:

With respect to CRYSVITA, although we are not aware of any other products currently in clinical development by a competitor for the treatment of XLH and TIO, it is possible that competitors may produce, develop, and commercialize therapeutics, or utilize other approaches such as gene therapy, to treat XLH and TIO. Most pediatric patients with XLH are managed using oral phosphate replacement and/or vitamin D therapy, which is relatively inexpensive and therefore may adversely affect our ability to commercialize CRYSVITA, if approved, in some countries.

This excerpt indicates that the coast appears clear for CRYSVITA in terms other disease modifying therapies over the near term. As for CRYSVITA's IP protection, Presentation Slide 40 provides a quick overview:

CRYSVITA gradually loses pieces of its protective matrix over the next 10 years. If I am correct in my assumption that the "Crysvita CoM patent" expiring in 2032 references its composition of matter patent, It appears to have strong colorable IP protection until then.

DOJOLVI

DOJOLVI (UX007/triheptanoin) is the second largest contributor to Ultragenyx's revenues, albeit it is guided to provide a mere fraction of Crysvita's aggregated revenues. The FDA approved DOJOLVI in 2020 as a source of calories and fatty acids for the treatment of pediatric and adult patients with confirmed long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorders (LC-FAOD).

At the time it was the first FDA approved treatment for LC-FAOD. DOJOLVI's current competitive situation is complex as described in Ultragenyx's 10-K. Most concerning are a raft of filed abbreviated new drug applications [ANDAs] seeking FDA approval to market a generic version of Dojolvi.

Based on DOJOLVI's exclusivity profile shown in Presentation slide 41 with its Dojolvi CoM Patent expiring in 2029, I would expect that Ultragenyx could forestall any such efforts to market a generic until at least 2029.

EVKEEZA

EVKEEZA (evinacumab-dgnb) was developed by Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (RGNX). Its FDA label shows that its initial U.S. approval came in 2021 for use by adults and children aged >12 years; it is an adjunct to other low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol [LDL-C] lowering therapies for the treatment of homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [HoFH]. Subsequently, it was approved by the FDA for children aged 5 to 11 in 2023 and then in 09/2025 for children from age 1 to less than 5 years old.

In 01/2022, Ultragenyx announced an in-license with Regeneron to commercialize EVKEEZA outside of the U.S. It then began the process of engaging country authorities within the EEA, Latin America, Canada and Japan to negotiate pricing and reimbursement guidelines.

The general terms of the license are stated in the extract below from Presentation slide 37:

During the Call CCO Harris reported as follows on EVKEEZA which he noted had only received reimbursement approval outside the U.S. in the last couple of years:

...In the EMEA region, we now have patients on reimbursed therapy from nearly all of the major countries, and we have added almost 100 patients since the beginning of the year with a total of approximately 285 patients across 15 countries. We continue to successfully navigate the country-by-country pricing negotiations and respond to named patient treatment requests across the whole EMEA region.

Our team in Japan continues to build on the launch momentum following the pricing and reimbursement approval that we received last year. In Canada, we are continuing pricing negotiations with government health authorities and recently added younger pediatric patients to the label. Over time, we expect Evkeeza revenue to contribute more meaningfully to the total revenue as we continue to successfully launch this transformative product for HoFH patients with its high potency due to a novel mechanism of action regardless of background therapy.

MEPSEVII

In terms of revenue generation MEPSEVII comes in nearly tied with EVKEEZA as a distant fourth. MEPSEVII (vestronidase alfa), was the first medicine approved for the treatment of children and adults with Mucopolysaccharidosis VII (MPS VII, Sly syndrome) in 2017.

In terms of competition, the 10-K is unable to cite any other compounds currently in clinical development for MPS VII. Slide 42 cites a MEPSEVII CoM Patent expiring in 2035.

As noted by CMO Crombez during the 09/2025 Morgan Stanley 23rd Annual Global Healthcare Conference, it is CRYSVITA not EVKEEZA or MEPSEVII which are driving the growth at Ultragenyx.

DTX401

Presentation slide 25 below provides a quick snapshot of Ultragenyx's next shot at an approved therapy:

DTX401 is slated for submission of its BLA to the FDA later in 2025 for treatment of glycogen storage disease type Ia [GSDIa], with launch expected in 2026. True to Ultragenyx's ticker symbol, GSDIa is quite rare with is ~6,000 patient prevalence in commercially accessible geographies.

The 10-K reveals a wide open near term competitive landscape for DTX401 as follows:

With respect to DTX401, there are currently no pharmacologic treatments for patients with GSDIa. We are aware of an mRNA therapy, mRNA-3745, in Phase 1 for GSDIa by Moderna.

As for DTX401's exclusivity, the 10-K notes:

We have a non-exclusive license from the National Institutes of Health, or NIH, to an issued U.S. patent expiring in 2034 (not accounting for any available PTE) and corresponding foreign patents covering a recombinant nucleic acid construct used in DTX401 that includes a codon-optimized version of the G6Pase gene.

Lacking patent exclusivity DTX401 Ultragenyx must rely on its orphan drug exclusivity for protection from generics.

UX111

In 12/2024 Ultragenyx filed its BLA for UX111 (ABO-102) AAV gene therapy as a treatment for patients with Sanfilippo syndrome type A [MPS IIIA]. In 07/2025 the FDA rejected its application with a CRL. The company's 07/2025 release announcing the CRL advised that FDA's referenced CMC issues were:

... readily addressable and many have already been addressed. While the CRL will delay the potential approval of UX111 to 2026, we are working with urgency to respond and resubmit...

Unfortunately the CRL is not only impacting UX111. It is also causing delay for DTX401 as noted by CMO Crombez during the Call where he noted:

... We want to proactively resolve any relevant CMC and facility questions that derive from the UX111 CRL that could possibly impact the DTX401 submission before we finalize the CMC section of the BLA. We expect to resolve the observations over the next few months and then finalize the BLA submission.

Ultragenyx has significant liquidity but only a modest cash runway

As stated during the Call as of 06/30/2025, Ultragenyx had $539 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities; this included $80 million of net proceeds raised through its ATM facility.

The 10-K sets out Ultragenyx's imposing totals of cash burn as reflected by its annual net cash used in operating activities totaling $380,465 for 2022, $474,806 for 2023 and $414,188 for 2024. Its cash burn for H1/2025 shown in its 10-Q is $274,748. This suggests that its cash runway beyond H2/2025 will be ~two quarters. In other words investors should expect ongoing financings.

If Ultragenyx can manage to secure approval of UX111, DTX401, and/or UX143 before the program sunsets in 09/2026, it will earn cash relief by monetizing priority review vouchers [PRV] as planned per Presentation slide 33. Each PRV it can cash in should extend its cash runway by ~quarter.

Conclusion

Ultragenyx is expected to report its Q3/2025 earnings after market close on 11/07/2025. Insofar as its Presentation dated 09/2025 includes its 2025 guidance for CRYSVITA and DOJOLVI revenues, I do not expect any surprises on that front, albeit as noted during the Call CRYSVITA revenues can be lumpy from quarter to quarter "primarily due to uneven ordering patterns for Crysvita in Latin America".

Much more interesting and with significant potential to impact its share's pricing will be its latest advice on its progress with its CMC issues as they impact UX111 and DTX401. Also impactful will be any updates it can give on its other late stage programs - UX143 for osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) and GTX-102 for Angelman syndrome (AS).

As I write on 10/16/2025 Ultragenyx is experiencing a serious rough patch. With its two nearest term prospects in limbo and its dicy liquidity I rate it as "Hold". It bears watching as a potential recovery candidate if it can show significant progress on its late stage candidates.

data.title: Betterware: Mexican Cash Cow Trading Like A Cyclical Trap
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Betterware (NYSE:BWMX) is an overlooked high-yield stock that's a perfect fit if you want to expose your portfolio to Mexico's discretionary consumer environment. Real wage gains, healthy employment levels, and monetary easing should continue to drive lower-middle-class discretionary and essential spending to high levels by post-pandemic standards.

Structurally, both Betterware and Jafra operate under an asset-light model, with extremely high margins and an EBITDA-to-FCF conversion rate of ~60% over the long term. The stock pays a hefty 9% yield, which, as you'll read in the article, has been well-covered by FCF every year since FY 2020.

The model is highly replicable and can be expanded to several markets in South America and the Caribbean with minimal investment. Jafra also operates in the United States, but as of this writing, it is not profitable.

Even with potentially heavier competition from Natura (OTCPK:NTCOY), which will focus on Latin America with a leaner model, I see a potential 40–50% return for the stock in 1–2 years.

Explaining the Macro Setup

Will Mexico experience a lost decade?

This is one of the most intriguing questions to ask when you look at a business linked to the discretionary spending of the country's lower and middle classes, like Betterware.

After all, contrary to what many think, it is definitely not a defensive stock, even though its business model is almost entirely asset-light with outsourced production and distribution.

We'll get to that. But to open this article, I'd like to show you what the 'Mexican Bears' arguments are for the 2020s:

1) Structurally, the Mexican economy is going through a difficult period. The first is the secular decline in GDP, which has never again managed to match the growth rate of the 1970s (growing in the low single digits for most of the 1990s and 2000s), and is projected to remain between 1.8% and 2.8% in 2026. The government deficit is the highest in the last decade (~5.7% of GDP).

2) ~75% of its GDP is based on foreign trade, which could be severely affected by the escalation of the trade war with the United States. Mass deportations also reduce remittances sent to the country (reducing discretionary spending for poorer families). Many companies would need to return to a model focused on domestic trade, which could lead to massive layoffs in specific sectors.

3) Industrial production is falling by 3.6% year-over-year in August 2025, the lowest reading since 2021. To top it off, Gross Fixed Investment is also down.

But aren't there positive signs?

I mean, I told you in another article that I wasn't bullish on Mexico, but some tailwinds were still blowing. Betterware may not be as defensive as Tiendas 3B (TBBB), but they share one thing: they both benefit from a lower/middle class with real wage gains.

And, in that sense, there are some reasons to feel more comfortable investing there, especially in retail.

There has been a ~120% increase in the minimum wage since the pandemic (from MXN 123.20 per day to MXN 278.90), a growth well above inflation, giving the lower class a real wage gain of about 25%. This was the highest real wage growth rate for OECD countries and is estimated to have lifted 13 million people out of poverty.

Even though minimum wages have increased, the unemployment rate remains at a healthy 2.9% in August 2025 (below the pre-pandemic level). The participation rate—although slightly below the 10-year average excluding outliers—is not at its post-2021 low.

Despite being sticky, September's inflation rate of 4.3% is not much different from pre-pandemic levels.

Banxico is in the midst of a rate-cutting cycle that began in early 2024. This could further accelerate discretionary spending by low- and middle-income groups.

And it's no wonder that ANTAD reported a nominal total growth in retail same-store sales during Q2 2025 compared to the same period last year (despite Q1 2025 performance lagging year-over-year in every month excluding January).

Now that I've given you both sides of the coin—thesis and antithesis—let's dig deeper into Betterware (more recently BeFra).

Betterware and Its Misunderstood Business Model

Starting from the basics, it's important to distinguish Betterware Mexico (acquired in 2001 by Luis Campos, a former Chairman of Tupperware Americas and therefore with extensive experience in DTC sales) from its former parent company in the UK.

The structure we know today began in early 2020 when Betterware Mexico went public through a SPAC. At this point, Betterware became the first Mexican company to list directly on Nasdaq.

After going public, the Campos family remains the controlling shareholder through the company Campalier (they hold ~54% of Betterware's shares).

Two years after going public, the company acquired Jafra—a beauty and personal care products company with a business model complementary to Betterware's both in Mexico and now in the United States.

The deal cost ~$255 million, and the payment was financed with debt (approximately $237.2 million of net debt issued that year) and the remainder with cash.

With Jafra now in the portfolio and a footprint in LatAm and North America, some changes have been made to governance:

  1. Andres Campos, son of Luis Campos, became CEO of the BeFra Group (now corresponding to Betterware and Jafra);
  2. Luis Campos became the Chairman;
  3. Santiago Campos (Luis's other son) left his position as CTO to take on the role of CEO of Betterware Mexico.

You already know the structure, let's dig into the business model.

As I mentioned before, Betterware operates an asset-light DTC sales model based on multi-level marketing. They essentially sell products (mostly purchased from suppliers in China) at a discount to these distributors, who then sell them to associates at a higher price.

There's a recruitment network that offers incentives for distributors to bring new resellers into the business, making it highly replicable and scalable in countries with a high rate of self-employed workers, such as those in Latin America.

On the other hand, Betterware doesn't need to open its own stores. Typically, its assets are simply regional distribution centers like the one in Zapopan.

Like Tiendas 3B, Betterware also benefits from the minimum wage increase. Both companies have reduced workforces but benefit from the real income gains of lower-class workers.

Translating this into financial language:

1) Betterware manages to achieve high margins even selling products to the lower-income segment of the population. The key here is the markup they achieve by purchasing from suppliers and selling to distributors. To give you an idea, the company's gross margin is ~66.9%, much higher than any Consumer Staples and at least 73% higher than the median for Consumer Discretionary.

2) They have very high EBITDA-to-OCF convertibility and, especially, EBITDA-to-FCF (historical conversion of ~60% according to the CFO in the last transcript). In addition to low operating leverage and small working capital requirements, what stands out is their minimal Capex (remember, only for DCs and similar things), making them a cash cow.

The acquisition of Jafra only increased the scope of the business, also strengthening the cross-selling of the existing distributor base (that is, in addition to reselling Betterware home products, they will also be able to resell cosmetics), increasing the average ticket per reseller.

Does this mean the business model is bulletproof? Far from it. There are some risks worth considering:

1) Jafra will likely face significant challenges expanding in the United States, as it is a market with intense competition from large retailers. Access to premium brand products is greater among the lower and middle classes, and incentives would need to be dollarized, making them more costly for lower penetration.

2) Negative perception of multi-level marketing (it is often seen as some kind of Ponzi Scheme). As I said before, the end customer experience is something that both Betterware and Jafra can't entirely control (only one of Kotler's 5 Ps, which is the product). Price, promotion, people, and place are the responsibility of the resellers, which can make the experience uneven.

3) There's a significant need to keep the number of resellers and distributors growing. Even a tailwind if there's moderate unemployment (more people trying to earn extra income), but overall, the number of buyers also drives the number of resellers. So, I think we can assume that a general slowdown will hurt Betterware quite a bit (and perhaps Jafra even more so, given the discretionary nature of the products it sells).

Natura + Avon = Big Challenge for Jafra

Something that made headlines recently was Natura's strategic recentralization in Latin America, abandoning unprofitable regions like Europe, Asia, and Africa.

The situation had been unsustainable since they decided to go global and acquired Avon. The last two years have seen net losses and negative OCF, with total revenues just over half of what they were post-acquisition.

Natura is one of Jafra's main competitors in DTC cosmetics and skincare sales, especially in Mexico (they are estimated to have over 500,000 representatives, an impressive growth of ~56% in four years). To give you an idea, when Jafra was acquired, the global network had ~443,000 representatives.

Natura is likely larger than Jafra in Mexico today.

The closure of older factories (such as the one in Interlagos, São Paulo) and the concentration in Cajamar, which has better highway access, suggest they will also need to work on optimizing their logistics structure.

All of this suggests that Natura will now redirect all its investments into marketing, digitalization, and consultant expansion in the same markets where Jafra operates (excluding the United States, of course).

And that's terrible. With a leaner capital structure and a robust portfolio, Natura will be able to offer better benefits, potentially taking a chunk out of Jafra's resellers. After all, they have over $400 million in cash + ST Investments, as well as a production plant (Celaya) in Mexico.

Is this likely to halt Jafra's growth in the country? Well, I'd say no, but it should slow it down.

Looking at recent trends, Jafra Mexico is showing very strong results.

Brand investments and product reformulations during Q2 not only increased the average check by over 9% but also expanded revenue (+10.9% year-over-year) and EBITDA margin (21.2%).

I expect this strength to continue in the short/medium term, but in general we already need to consider some margin erosion and increased competition in markets like Mexico and other countries where the company is looking to expand.

Monstrous Yield, But Is It Sustainable?

Something that stands out about Betterware is its gigantic dividend yield, currently at 9%. If we look at the average over the last four years, it's 10.9%. This is about 402% higher than the median for Consumer Discretionary.

But if we look a little deeper, we'll notice that these payments have been quite irregular since the company went public four years ago.

Currently, Betterware pays $0.29 to its shareholders each quarter ($1.16 annualized). However, in FY 2021, they used to pay almost double that, ~$0.48 per share quarterly.

I confess that it's quite difficult to predict how much they'll pay annually. Currently, the payout is ~83% of EPS. With 37.2 million shares (a float of ~17 million if we disregard the controlling interest), the total annual dividend return is approximately $43.15 million.

Interestingly, while inconsistent, Betterware has generated FCF capable of paying this in every year since it went public.

Just take a look:

Even though in certain years the payout exceeded 100%, looking at the FCF (the most accurate measure to determine something about dividends), it seems well covered to me.

But what about going forward?

Well, we have some numbers to dig into here.

Starting with the guidance, management is targeting MXN 14.9–15.3 billion and EBITDA of MXN 2.9–3 billion for FY 2025 as a whole. Converting this to US dollars, we're talking about revenue of $805–827 million and EBITDA of $156.7–162.1 million.

If we simply use the ~60% EBITDA-to-FCF conversion rate we mentioned earlier, we could expect FCF of $94–97.2 million for FY 2025. Essentially, a very strong 21.3% FCF growth year over year.

Once again, Betterware would be comfortable with a 9% yield. There would even be around $50 million left for deleveraging.

Looking further ahead, in FY 2026, Wall St. expects revenue of $842.6 million. If we assume that the EBITDA margin compresses to ~15% given the competition from Jafra in Mexico and the sluggishness in the United States (currently at 17% and the 5-year average is 22.3%), we could target an EBITDA of $126.4 million.

Applying the EBITDA-to-FCF conversion rate of 60%, in this case, we would have an FCF of $75.8 million. Would again be able to cover the current 9% yield, but with less leeway.

I have a few considerations to make:

1) Jafra US is weighing on consolidated EBITDA. If they withdraw from this market (which, in my opinion, could become a drain on resources), Betterware could approach its 5-year EBITDA margin, as Jafra Mexico is a strong cash generator.

2) Management still doesn't seem to be counting on a stronger erosion from Natura/Avon mergers in Latin America. If we see margins of ~20% for FY 2026, as they are projecting for FY 2025, FCF could reach $101.1 million, continuing the upward trend.

3) In August, Betterware refinanced its remaining debt, likely at lower interest rates. Its balance sheet is healthy, and I see no reason why deleveraging would interfere with dividend payments at this point.

All in all, I will consider a mid-to-high single-digit shareholder yield as the benchmark for total return at the end of this thesis.

Risks to the Thesis

I see several risks to my thesis on Betterware.

The first is macro risk.

As you've read so far, my projection is that the lower and middle classes will continue to benefit from real income gains while inflation remains moderate and unemployment remains low.

However, there is a risk that key sectors for the country—such as manufacturing, for example—will suffer job cuts due to escalating tensions with the United States regarding tariffs. Mass layoffs could occur as labor costs rise.

This is a macro risk, as Betterware is not as affected by rising labor costs and even benefits from moderate unemployment (more people looking to earn extra income are becoming resellers).

Another significant risk is Natura/Avon eating into Jafra's market by focusing on Latin America.

They have their own production plants and could invest more heavily in marketing, eroding Jafra's market share in Mexico. A potential margin erosion could even eat into some of the hefty dividends they pay today, largely undermining the reason this stock is attractive.

And of course, exchange rate/political risks are always present when targeting a Latin American cash cow, but you should already know that by now.

Is Betterware Cheap or Yield Trap?

Looking solely at FCF yield, we'd be looking at something like ~13%. This is incredibly high, even for foreign stocks with currency risk and a very high payout.

We can compare Betterware based on its historical multiples and with some DTC personal care/home goods peers. I believe a SOTP approach is ideal for Betterware.

For the entire FY 2025, I'll use the same contribution ratios from Betterware and Jafra to revenue and EBITDA.

Thus, we can model:

  • Betterware Mexico would represent approximately 42% of revenue and 63% of EBITDA. This means we're targeting $342.7 million in revenue and $100.4 million in EBITDA.
  • Jafra Mexico and the US are expected to generate approximately 58% of revenue and 37% of EBITDA. Therefore, I target $473.2 million in revenue and $58.9 million in EBITDA.

Starting with Jafra, I separated a group of peers consisting of Natura, Herbalife (HLF), USANA (USNA), and Nu Skin (NUS). Within these peers, I separated current and historical multiples.

Thus, targeting the revenue I already mentioned, we could arrive at an EV of $320.5 million (revenue) and $334.8 million (EBITDA). The average of both gives us an EV of $327.6 million.

Looking at Betterware, I prefer to use historical multiples. I couldn't find any peer that doesn't inflate comparisons well above the levels at which the stock typically trades.

That said, I'll be targeting ~6.6x for EBITDA (5-year average) and ~0.75x for EV/S (in line with Consumer Discretionary and a 20% discount given currency and geographic risks).

In this sense, we would be facing an EV of $662.6 million (EBITDA) and $257 million (revenue). The average of these is around $459.8 million.

Adding both together, we would arrive at an EV of ~$786.8 million. Removing the net debt of $251.2 million (LT Debt + ST Debt) and adjusting for shares outstanding, we would arrive at a target price of ~$14.50.

Essentially, an upside of 15% from where the stock is trading today.

My price target is conservative compared to Wall St.'s ($18.67), but I believe it reflects the very low multiples at which the market views this type of business model.

Taking a quick look, if we put everything in the same basket and aim for 6.6x EBITDA (historical multiple), we would arrive at a price target of ~$21 for this stock.

Are we undervaluing Jafra Mexico by comparing it to less profitable DTCs? After all, it generates an EBITDA margin of approximately 20% and high cash convertibility, far above the peers I selected for it.

For better or worse, as a final method, I prefer to average these two price targets ($14.50 and $21).

As my final price target, I'll settle on ~$17.75. In this scenario, I don't set the same multiples for Jafra as Betterware, but I also don't undervalue them as I did the first time.

At this point, we'd have an upside of ~40% from here. Adding the shareholder yield, the total return would reach almost 50%. Not bad.

That said, my rating on Betterware is 'Buy'.

It's a high-yielding Mexican cash cow with its risks: cyclical, with currency risk, high payout, and it should face tougher competition going forward. The macro environment seems stable to me for discretionary consumption as long as real wage gains still exist at this magnitude.

Just to estimate the downside here, let's use my conservative projection for FY 2026, with Natura eating into the company's margins (EBITDA of $126.4 million), and using the same 6.6x multiple, we would arrive at a price target of $13.60 already discounted to present value.

So, even with the recent rally, the market is pricing in an even greater decline in EBITDA margin than my scenario for FY 2026. If we count on the dividend yield in the mid to high single digits, this scenario is not very worrying yet.

Conclusion

Although not defensive, Betterware is a cyclical high-yield stock enjoying macro tailwinds and very high cash generation.

It's understandable that, given its DTC structure and multi-level marketing, the market trades it at a discount to the Consumer Discretionary median. But at this point, the discount appears to have passed its limit.

In a scenario where Mexico avoids a lost decade, Betterware could deliver total returns close to 50%, clearly justifying the 'Buy' rating here.

These are strong returns for patient investors seeking emerging-market income and some expansion optionality.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Marriott International: Not In The Buying Zone Yet
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I'm continuing to explore the investment appeal of hotels. Having covered Hilton (HLT) and Accor (OTCPK:ACRFF) in a previous article, I decided to take a look at Marriott International (NASDAQ:MAR). I personally haven't stayed at Marriott as often as I have at Hilton, but I can't ignore the world's largest hotel chain with over 1.6 million rooms. After a thorough review of its financial statements and fundamentals, I have concluded that the company is very similar in model and performance to Hilton, which is why it has also been assigned a hold rating.

Thesis Disclaimer

I use a hold rating, but my approach would be better served by a rating scale from avoid to attractive. Neutral status or hold rating relative to MAR stock is based solely on an analysis of the financial performance of the business without an assessment of the current value, although this analysis will also be carried out towards the end of the article. My opinion may be useful to investors considering whether to include the stock in a long-term portfolio and hold it for a 3-5 year horizon, and it does not constitute a recommendation or trading instruction.

Thesis Explanation

I strive to avoid bias or subjectivity when evaluating investments, so each stock is evaluated using my quantitative model and assigned a rating. This rating is adjusted for industry specifics or other factors, where possible and appropriate.

After evaluating financial performance metrics, solvency, and actual and future growth rates, I arrived at a score of 68 points (or percentages) out of a possible 100, which is just 2 points below the threshold for an attractive stock. This is a situation where precision can be compromised if there's a reason to do so.

This is an average rating, and it justifies the moderate HOLD rating. Marriott's business is successful in terms of efficiency and performance and is sustainable in terms of debt and solvency, but there are questions about the sustainability of earnings growth.

Since I compared Hilton and Accor in my previous article, and the first mentioned emerged victorious in the virtual stock battle, I can afford to compare it with Marriott as well. Moreover, the companies have somewhat similar business approaches: an asset-light model with a focus on franchise fees.

Marriott Is Just As Successful In Franchising As Hilton

The vast majority of hotels are owned not by Marriott, but by franchisees or management partners. The company receives stable franchise and management fees and does not incur capital expenditures for construction and maintenance. According to the Q2 2025 report, the net share of franchise fees in revenue, excluding cost reimbursement revenue, is 61%. However, there are two other items. Base management fees are fixed payments that Marriott receives from hotel owners for management services (operations, marketing, reservations, quality standards). Incentive management fees are variable payments that Marriott receives in addition to base fees, but only if the hotel achieves a certain level of profitability. They are usually calculated as a percentage of the hotel's operating profit after covering fixed expenses. Together with these items, the share of revenue from the owned portion of the hotel chain is only 23%, but this is several percentage points higher than Hilton's.

Another minor difference is a more prominent presence in the luxury segment and greater geographic diversification. Effective segmentation ensures high RevPAR (revenue per available room), which, coupled with an asset-light approach, ensures high margins.

Net margin above 36%, significant ROIC of 16% calculated using the Mauboussin and Callahan method, and earning quality above 100% lead to ideal financial performance. An outstanding business with a competitive advantage.

Solvent With Negative Equity

Due to its larger scale and the higher proportion of company-owned hotels, Marriott's average debt over the past four quarters is higher than HLT's – $15.5 billion versus $11.8 billion. However, Marriott's operating profit is such that the key solvency metric I look at is within acceptable limits and better than its competitor's. This is interest coverage: according to my calculations, debt servicing accounts for 19% of operating profit (TTM), so the ratio is 5.28. For me, 5 serves as the threshold between "everything is under control" and "there are some potential problems".

However, there are real problems from the quantitative analysis approach. This is negative equity. Fidgeting with the borrowing structure and share buybacks leads us to a capital deficit of almost $3 billion as of June 2025. While I don't use the classic debt-to-equity metric in my model, in the article "Booking Holdings: Too Expensive For Such A Risk," I wrote about why I'm afraid of negative equity and that it's a clear warning sign for me to be extremely cautious.

Moderate But Justified Growth

Annual room growth of 3-5%, expansion into emerging markets with potentially higher profit margins, and a focus on the luxury segment are the main drivers of future net profit growth. According to analysts' forecasts, earnings per share can be targeted at $13 by December 2027, representing a CAGR of ~12.5%.

In addition to organic growth and the search for higher RevPAR, the company is working to diversify its sources of cash flow. I would highlight the launch of Marriott Media, which will enable efforts to engage hotel guests and further monetize loyalty program audiences. It is also worth noting the strong pipeline portfolio of 300 projects in the branded residence sector. This is a strengthening of the asset portfolio and high-margin direction.

The entire travel industry, starting from a low base in 2020, has demonstrated a strong recovery over the past five years, entering a cycle of sustainable growth. MAR is no exception.

However, despite the factors listed above, I can't consider growth a strength of MAR. The three-year trend is worse than the five-year baseline, and future growth rates are lower than the last cycle and lower than Hilton's (12.5% ​​versus 14%). This is likely why MAR has historically been valued more cheaply.

My Two Cents About Valuation

The current valuation of 27.2x compared to Hilton's valuation of 34.6x seems quite low, but this comparison is misleading. As I said, Marriott has historically been at a discount to its competitor. I'm using historical multiples method to see how the current price is aligned with the entry point. The median price-to-earnings ratio over the past three years as of the quarterly report date is 24.5x, according to my calculations.

With current earnings per share of $9.68 TTM, this corresponds to a price of ~$233. In fact, I can conclude that, by my estimate, Marriott is overvalued by ~12%, trading even above the median of the top third of estimates, which equals $261 per share.

Risks To The Thesis

Although my valuation factors currently place me outside the market, Marriott shares will definitely make my shortlist for medium-term monitoring. The risks of investing in Marriott are, in fact, typical for any company in this industry, and they can be divided into three groups:

  1. Macroeconomics: A global economic slowdown could hit business travel and the luxury segment as well.
  2. Geopolitics: Tensions in Eastern Europe and the Middle East are not conducive to the industry's development in these and adjacent regions, and are also leading to transaction costs and rising prices for related goods. The trade war with China is also a potential pressure factor.
  3. Competition: Hilton, Accor, and alternative models like Airbnb offer value as well.

Bottom Line

Marriott boasts a combination of global scale, a sustainable asset-light model, and a strong loyalty program. With projected EPS growth of 12-14% in the coming years and active buybacks, the shares remain attractive to long-term investors, although the return potential will largely depend on the global economic cycle. But to make a balanced and informed decision, I resort to a quantitative assessment of both the business itself and its market valuation.

Having calculated my own quant rating and estimated fair value, I conclude that buying at the current price is, at the very least, not consistent with the cautious investor's strategy. Therefore, I will update my opinion in one or two quarters.

data.title: iRobot: Hope Keeps The Company's Volatility High
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to go through iRobot Corporation’s (NASDAQ:IRBT) performance since the last time I covered it back in January of this year to see if there has been any substantial progress in staying alive. However, it still looks like the end may be imminent unless it continues to find ways to continue to delay its term loan payments further and manages to pivot into something a lot more doable and competitive - which such pivot will take a lot of effort, and resources.

Briefly on Performance

Sales over the last three quarters continue their long-term downward trajectory with no signal of slowing down. For the next quarter, analysts expect IRBT will do $136m in sales. That translates to around a 30% y/y decline.

In terms of profitability, the company continues to bleed, with most of the margins being deep in the red, with slight improvements in the latest quarter. At least its gross margins are still positive. Even using non-GAAP metrics like EBITDA, the company still cannot turn profitable.

What about its balance sheet? IRBT finished with $40.5m in cash against no long-term debt, but with around $203m in current debt. Looking into this debt further, the term loan was due on September 19th, 2025. On the company’s 10Q report, we can see that there are concerns of “substantial doubt about the Company’s ability to continue as a going concern.”

Luckily for IRBT, its loan covenants were extended to October 24th, which is about a week from now. At this point, the debt is a significant risk and may mean that the company can potentially default on its debt very soon. The company has been issuing equity-linked warrants to the lenders as partial compensation and to secure further covenant waivers, which will result in dilution risk for shareholders.

Additionally, the company's available cash and restricted cash of around $35m for debt repayment amounts to around $76m, while its cash flow from operations was -$56m, which implies that the company's monthly cash burn rate is close to $10m. This means that the company could be in huge financial trouble sometime in February of next year if nothing improves until then.

In short, the company’s performance is not looking great, and bankruptcy could well be on its doorstep pretty soon if things do not improve.

Comments on the Outlook

I wanted to take a look at some of the good and bad regarding the company’s position as a going concern.

Since the last time I covered iRobot back in January of this year, there has been quite a lot of volatility in the company’s shares, which mostly came from hope and had nothing to do with any fundamental change in the company. Robotics companies got a slight pick over the last year, and since IRBT literally has robot in its name, it got swept away by that FOMO, too.

The truth is, IRBT is not trading on any fundamentals and hasn’t been trading like that in forever. For now, it will be one of those stocks that will see a spike in its share price with any positive news, no matter how insignificant it is, mainly because it’s cheap right now, and many people love trading meme stocks ever since the GameStop (GME) fiasco of 2021.

The company is losing ground in robot vacuums to other companies that offer a better price and a decent product, like Roborock and Ecovacs, as well as the more established peers like Dyson and SharkNinja. There is a lot of competition, and it is clearly eating IRBT’s lunch right now.

A lot of these spikes in the company’s share, as I mentioned, were from some hope-driven momentum, followed by some shorts covering. 26% of the company’s shares are currently sold short, so if there is some momentum behind a move, you’d better believe there will be some covering happening to bring the share price up even more. The most recent example was when the company’s Chief Marketing Officer, Athena Kasvikis, said she sees a “really interesting growth phase,” and that “it’s all upside for iRobot.” A marketing employee would say that about the company’s prospects, but it is all just words right now. Nevertheless, the company’s shares skyrocketed 55% after these comments, showing how traders are looking for any hope.

In the long run, the longer-term business challenges remain. The weakening product demand, massive market competition, and even currency risks.

A pivot is in order

In my opinion, a pivot is what the company needs right now, if it is to survive at all. Athena mentioned that the technology now integrates AI cameras on a lot of their new robots, which are there to assist humans even more. The company needs to take the technology it has and expand its AI capabilities further into integrated robotics. The company’s ClearView Lidar Navigation, which enhances robot autonomy and user experience, should be utilized even more, beyond robot vacuums. Uses like lawn care robots, home security, and even elder care assistance, and integrated IoT smart home ecosystems. Relying solely on a robot vacuum is not going to work, especially when the competition is a lot more diversified, and if their robot vacuum sales plummet, it is just one of the segments.

If the company can survive until robotics, coupled with AI integration, becomes a lot more prominent, and the company manages to pivot or expand its product portfolio, it could work out.

Future Partnerships would bring a lot more hope

The most-watched segment right now is AI and anything related to it. If the management can secure a decent partnership with one of the leading AI companies right now, you should expect the company’s shares to probably triple in a day, if, for example, the company somehow secures a partnership with the AI darling, Nvidia (NVDA), or close seconds like Microsoft (MSFT), or Amazon (AMZN) for their cloud AI and IoT technologies.

This is all just ideas I pulled out of thin air, but any company that announces some sort of partnership with one of the big dogs gets a boost, which, if you are a long-term investor still believing in the company, could provide a way out for less profit, or breakeven, or even a gain, depending on when you started the position. If comments from its CMO can send the company’s shares up 55% on the day, an actual fundamental change in operations with a substantial partnership could be the ticket out. That seems a little far-fetched if you ask me, but one can always dream.

Unfortunately, the company hasn’t seemed to host any conference calls since mid-2024, which isn’t very investor-friendly. Thus, we don't have much to go by in what the management thinks about the company’s ability to stay afloat.

Key Takeaways

I think it is quite interesting to follow the company’s progress at this point in its operations. It is akin to an explosion happening, and you can’t turn away. I am following the company mostly for the drama right now and questioning whether the company can find a way to claw out of this hole. Unfortunately for the company, the AMZN deal did not come true, so now we get to continue to enjoy the show.

Will it find a way to pay back its loans? Is it going to survive? I would actually like to see the company pivoting hard and find success in the future, but right now, the odds are stacked against it.

data.title: Is Bitcoin's Popularity Undermining Its Use For Hedging Stock Market Risk?
data.description:
By James Picerno

Bitcoin may or may not become a global standard for digital payments in the years ahead, but it looks like a good proxy for assessing the financial market’s appetite for risk.

That’s good news for investors looking for an alternative read on equity risk sentiment, but it could be problematic for using bitcoin as a hedge against stock market risk.

Back in March we noted that comparing the most popular cryptocurrency with US shares (S&P 500 Index) suggests an increasingly tighter connection between bitcoin and sentiment on Wall Street. Seven months later, the connection between the two asset classes looks even tighter.

The latest example: When stocks tanked last Friday (Oct. 10), so did bitcoin, which lost substantially more that day vs. the S&P 500. Gold, by contrast, was up 1.0%.

One day could be noise, of course, but a review of bitcoin vs. stocks over the last several years confirms a tighter connection between the assets in recent history. For example, over the past three years, the daily returns for bitcoin and the S&P 500 posted a moderately positive 0.33 correlation, up from a slightly negative correlation in the previous three-year period.

Note: Perfect correlation (no diversification benefit) is indicated by 1.0; no correlation (random behavior) is 0.0; and -1.0 is perfect negative correlation (always moving inversely).

Although the cryptocurrency still exhibits a fair amount of return independence from stocks, the degree of low/negative correlation is fading as the asset moves into the mainstream of financial markets.

Monitoring risk sentiment

The upside of a higher correlation: there are times when bitcoin appears to offer contemporaneous, or early, signals of a change in risk sentiment for stocks.

The digital asset’s downturn in early 2025, for instance, foreshadowed the subsequent slide in equities. Coincidence? Maybe, but something similar occurred again in April, when bitcoin bottomed around the time that the decline in stocks started to reverse.

These two events suggest that this crypto asset sometimes confirms, if not predicts, a change in investors’ risk appetite for stocks.

By contrast, bitcoin in its early days was seen as an independent asset, disconnected from traditional financial markets and the economy. That perception was largely attributable to the cryptocurrency ecosystem, which, historically, operated outside the mainstream of finance through a network of independent and predominantly offshore exchanges.

But the growing embrace of the cryptocurrency by institutions and easier access for individuals via publicly traded bitcoin funds have tightened the ebb and flow between the two asset classes.

Correlation and Diversification

The rising correlation between bitcoin and stocks suggests that the digital currency has become somewhat less useful for diversifying equity risk, if only on the margins so far. Consider how a rolling 3-month window for daily returns has evolved.

The linear trend shows the correlation approaching 0.4 (black line in the chart below). That’s still well below correlations between, say, US large-cap stocks vs. small-caps, or US stocks and foreign equities. But if recent history is a guide, bitcoin and stocks look set to become even more closely aligned in the future.

Bitcoin’s moderately positive correlation still offers a degree of independence from stocks, but the connection between the two assets is showing stronger alignment in periods of elevated stock market stress, such as during the spring correction.

Bitcoin’s story as an investment class has continually evolved, from digital cash to a store of value, and later to a volatile, high-return vehicle. As its correlation with equity markets deepens, it is beginning to resemble a speculative asset more than the alternative store of value it was once touted to be.

It’s too soon to write off bitcoin as a diversifying force for stocks. Even if it’s more closely aligned with equities going forward, it may still be useful as a complement for diversifying stock market risk vis-a-vis other asset classes, such as bonds, commodities and property shares.

But if 2025 is a guide, it seems that the crypto’s success in going mainstream is starting to conflict with its earlier image as an asset apart from the financial system’s status quo.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Visa's Valuation Looks High - Until You Run The Numbers
data.description:
